Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.

Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.

When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Good, the character moved further right. Now I can see the character is getting closer to the green shape. Let me check the current position and plan the remaining moves to get the character into the green shape.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current character position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
white = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'W'][0]
green = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N'][0]
print(f"White boundary: {white['boundary']}")
print(f"Green boundary: {green['boundary']}")

# Get approximate centers
white_cols = [p[1] for p in white['boundary']]
white_rows = [p[0] for p in white['boundary']]
green_cols = [p[1] for p in green['boundary']]
green_rows = [p[0] for p in green['boundary']]

print(f"\nWhite: rows {min(white_rows)}-{max(white_rows)}, cols {min(white_cols)}-{max(white_cols)}")
print(f"Green: rows {min(green_rows)}-{max(green_rows)}, cols {min(green_cols)}-{max(green_cols)}")

# The green shape has a hole (sky blue interior). Let me check the hole position
hole = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S' and n['id'] != 0][0] if any(n['color'] == 'S' and n['id'] != 0 for n in seg['nodes']) else None
if hole:
    print(f"Hole: {hole['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Hmm, the white node now has boundary [[0, 16], [0, 18]] which is at the top of the screen - that's the dark gray bar area. Wait, that's wrong. Let me re-check. The white character should be at a different position. Let me look at all nodes again.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Something's off - let me list all nodes
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    print(f"  id={node['id']} color={node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary'][:4]}...")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see:
- The white character (id=3) is at cols 29-43, rows 11-19. It's close to the green shape!
- The green shape (id=6) is at rows 29-49, cols 41-49
- The hole in the green shape (id=7) is at rows 32-46, cols 44-46
- The red feet are at rows 26-28, cols 32-34 and 38-40
The character needs to go DOWN to reach the green shape. The green shape's hole is at cols 44-46, and the character is at cols 29-43. So I need to move RIGHT a bit more and then DOWN.
Wait, looking at the image, the character seems to be right next to the green shape now. The character's right edge is at col 43 and the green shape starts at col 41. They might be overlapping or adjacent.
Let me try moving DOWN to get the character into the green shape.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Character is at cols 29-43, green shape at cols 41-49
# Need to move RIGHT a bit more and then DOWN
# Let me try DOWN first to see what happens
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
